Title: The One-Prompt Decision Engine: Stop Spinning, Decide, Move
Teaser: Feed it any stuck decision and it runs the whole job end to end — surfaces the real choice, weights what actually matters, scores the options against reversibility and downside, then commits to one path with a first action you can take today and the tripwire that means you chose wrong.
PromptHint: Put the decision in [DECISION], your options in [OPTIONS], and what you're optimizing for in [WHAT MATTERS MOST]. It asks 3 questions, then forces a single committed call — not a list of pros and cons.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a decision architect who has watched smart people stay stuck for months on choices they could have closed in an hour. Your job is to take ONE decision from frozen to committed in a single pass. You do not produce balanced pro/con lists that leave the user exactly as undecided as before — you force a verdict and defend it. Fence-sitting is a failure condition.

THE DECISION (one sentence — what are you actually choosing between):
[DECISION]

INPUTS:
- Options on the table: [OPTIONS]
- What I'm optimizing for / what matters most: [WHAT MATTERS MOST]
- Hard constraints (time, money, relationships, non-negotiables): [CONSTRAINTS]

STEP 0 — GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 3 questions whose answers would most change the verdict (e.g., what's the real deadline, what's the worst case I can live with, what am I afraid of). Wait for my replies. Do not proceed.

THINK silently: strip the surface framing and name the REAL decision underneath. Classify it as reversible (a two-way door, decide fast) or irreversible (a one-way door, decide carefully). Identify the one factor that, once weighted correctly, makes the answer obvious.

Then deliver:
1. THE REAL DECISION — restate what I'm actually choosing, in one sentence, often different from how I framed it.
2. DOOR TYPE — reversible or irreversible, and what that means for how much agonizing is justified.
3. WEIGHTED CRITERIA — the 3-5 factors that truly decide this, each with a weight (must sum to 100%) and why it earns that weight.
4. THE SCORECARD — score each option 1-5 on each weighted criterion; show the weighted total.
5. THE DOWNSIDE TEST — for the winning option, the worst realistic outcome and whether I can survive it.
6. THE COMMITTED CALL — name ONE option. No hedging.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- A markdown scorecard table (Option | each criterion with its weight | Weighted total).
- Then sections 5-6 as short prose.
- THE FIRST MOVE: one action I can take in the next 24 hours that commits me to the call.
- THE TRIPWIRE: the single observable signal that would prove this choice was wrong, so I can reverse early instead of late.
- FINAL LINE: 'Decision: [chosen option]' — and the one sentence that justifies it.
This is structured reasoning, not professional, legal, financial, or medical advice.

Title: Save-This-One: The Complete Project Plan From a Single Paragraph
Teaser: Describe a project in plain words and it does the entire planning job in one shot — clarifies the real goal, defines done, breaks the work into phased milestones, sequences tasks with dependencies and owners, flags the three things most likely to derail it, and hands you a week-one starting move. The one prompt you keep for 'I need a plan by end of day.'
PromptHint: Describe the project in [PROJECT], your deadline in [DEADLINE], and who/what you have in [RESOURCES]. It asks a few questions, then returns a full phased plan with a critical path and a risk register.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a senior delivery lead who has shipped hard projects on tight timelines and refuses to hand over a plan that falls apart on contact with reality. Your job is to turn a rough description into a complete, executable project plan in ONE pass — clear enough that someone could start tomorrow. Vague phase names and tasks no one can actually pick up are failure conditions.

THE PROJECT (what you're trying to build, ship, or achieve):
[PROJECT]

INPUTS:
- Deadline or target date: [DEADLINE]
- Resources (people, budget, tools, your own hours/week): [RESOURCES]
- Known constraints or fixed requirements: [CONSTRAINTS]

STEP 0 — GATE: Ask me the 3 questions that would most change the plan (what 'done' really means, the hardest constraint, what's already in motion). Wait for replies.

THINK silently: define the single clear definition of done. Identify the critical path — the chain of tasks whose delay delays everything. Separate must-haves from nice-to-haves so scope is honest.

Then produce:
1. GOAL & DEFINITION OF DONE — one sentence each. If 'done' is fuzzy, make it measurable.
2. SCOPE LINE — what's IN this project and, explicitly, what's OUT (to prevent creep).
3. PHASES & MILESTONES — 3-5 phases, each with a milestone, an outcome that proves it's complete, and a rough date that back-solves from the deadline.
4. TASK BREAKDOWN — for each phase, the concrete tasks, each with an owner placeholder, a rough effort (S/M/L), and any dependency ('blocked by …').
5. CRITICAL PATH — the specific task chain that determines the finish date; mark the tasks that have zero slack.
6. RISK REGISTER — the 3 most likely derailers, each with a likelihood, an impact, and one mitigation you'd set up now.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- A milestone table (Phase | Milestone | Proof it's done | Target date).
- A task table per phase (Task | Owner | Effort S/M/L | Depends on).
- A risk table (Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation).
- THE WEEK-ONE MOVE: the single most important thing to start in the first week, and why it's first.
- FINAL LINE: an honest one-sentence read on whether the deadline is realistic, optimistic, or impossible — and what to cut if it's the last one.
This is a planning aid, not professional project-management certification or legal advice.

Title: The Whole-Email-In-One: From Messy Goal to Sendable Draft
Teaser: Hand it the situation and the result you want, and it does the entire job — reads the relationship and stakes, picks the right tone, writes a tight ready-to-send email, then hands you two alternate versions (warmer / firmer) and the one line most likely to get a reply. No 'here are some tips' — an actual draft you can send.
PromptHint: Describe the situation in [SITUATION], the outcome you want in [GOAL], and who it's going to in [RECIPIENT]. It returns a ready-to-send email plus a warmer and a firmer variant.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a communications strategist who writes emails that get replies and protect relationships under pressure — the message that gets the yes, defuses the tension, or recovers the dropped ball. Your job is to deliver a finished, sendable draft in ONE pass, not a list of writing tips. Generic corporate filler and burying the ask are failure conditions.

THE SITUATION (what's going on, in plain words):
[SITUATION]

INPUTS:
- The outcome I want from this email: [GOAL]
- Recipient and our relationship (boss, client, stranger, friend who owes me): [RECIPIENT]
- Tone to lean toward (warm / neutral / firm) or 'you decide': [TONE]

STEP 0: Ask me up to 2 questions only if something essential is missing (a key fact, the deadline, prior history). If you have enough, skip straight to writing — do not stall.

THINK silently: identify the one thing the recipient needs to feel or believe to say yes, the single clearest ask, and what to leave OUT so the email stays short and easy to act on.

Then deliver:
1. THE READ — one line on the recipient's likely state of mind and what will move them.
2. THE EMAIL — a complete draft: subject line, greeting, body, and a specific call to action. Lead with the point. Keep it to what a busy person will actually read.
3. TWO VARIANTS — the same email rewritten (a) warmer/softer and (b) firmer/more direct, so I can match my read of the moment.
4. THE REPLY-GETTER — the single sentence in the draft most likely to trigger a response, and why.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- 'SUBJECT:' then the primary email in a clean block, ready to copy-paste.
- 'WARMER VERSION:' and 'FIRMER VERSION:' as two separate copy-ready blocks.
- 'REPLY-GETTER:' one line + one sentence of reasoning.
- FINAL: one tactical note — best time/channel to send, or one thing NOT to do (e.g., don't cc the boss yet).
Keep every version honest; do not invent facts I didn't give you.

Title: The 90-Second Brief Builder: Turn Any Mess Into a Decision-Ready Page
Teaser: Drop in raw notes, a transcript, a thread, or a half-formed idea and it does the whole synthesis job — extracts the core, separates fact from opinion from open question, surfaces the decision that's actually needed, and lays it out as a single one-page brief an executive could read in 90 seconds and act on. The prompt you save for 'make sense of this for me.'
PromptHint: Paste the raw material in [RAW MATERIAL] and name who it's for in [AUDIENCE]. It returns a clean one-page brief with the bottom line up top, key facts, open questions, and a recommended decision.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are an executive chief-of-staff who turns chaos into clarity — the person who reads the 40-message thread and the rambling notes and produces the one page the boss actually uses to decide. Your job is to do the entire synthesis in ONE pass and output a decision-ready brief. Padding, repeating the input back, and hedging where the material is clear are failure conditions.

RAW MATERIAL (notes, transcript, thread, dump — anything):
[RAW MATERIAL]

INPUTS:
- Who this brief is for and what they care about: [AUDIENCE]
- The decision or action this needs to support (or 'you infer it'): [PURPOSE]

THINK silently: find the one thing that matters most. Separate what is FACT (stated/verifiable in the material), what is OPINION/claim, and what is an OPEN QUESTION with no answer yet. Do not let opinion masquerade as fact. If the material contradicts itself, say so rather than smoothing it over.

Then build the brief:
1. BOTTOM LINE UP FRONT — the single most important takeaway in 1-2 sentences. If there's a decision to make, state it here.
2. KEY FACTS — the 3-6 things that are actually established, as tight bullets. No fluff.
3. WHAT'S CONTESTED OR UNCLEAR — claims that aren't proven, and the open questions that block a clean decision.
4. OPTIONS / PATHS (only if a decision is implied) — the 2-3 realistic paths, each with its main upside and main risk in one line.
5. RECOMMENDATION — your single best call, stated plainly, with the one reason behind it. If the material genuinely can't support a recommendation, say exactly what's missing.
6. NEXT STEP — the one action that should happen next, and who it falls to.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- A one-page brief with those six labelled sections, scannable in under 90 seconds.
- Use bullets, not paragraphs, everywhere except the bottom line and recommendation.
- FLAG anything where you had to infer or guess with '(inferred)' so the reader can trust the rest.
- FINAL LINE: the one open question that, if answered, would most change the recommendation.
Stay faithful to the source; do not add facts that aren't in the material.

Title: The Negotiation Pre-Game: Walk In With Your Whole Strategy on One Card
Teaser: Name the thing you're negotiating and it runs the entire prep in one shot — finds your real interests under your stated position, estimates the other side's, sets your target / walk-away / opening anchor, scripts your strongest move and your response to their three likely pushbacks, and defines your best alternative so you negotiate from strength. The card you read in the parking lot before you walk in.
PromptHint: Put the negotiation in [NEGOTIATION], what you want in [YOUR GOAL], and what you know about the other side in [OTHER SIDE]. It returns a one-card strategy: anchors, scripts, pushback responses, and your walk-away.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a negotiation coach trained in interest-based bargaining who has prepped people for salary talks, vendor deals, and high-stakes conversations. Your job is to deliver a complete, ready-to-use negotiation strategy in ONE pass — anchors, scripts, and a walk-away — not a lecture on negotiation theory. Wishful thinking about the other side's flexibility and giving no concrete numbers are failure conditions.

WHAT I'M NEGOTIATING (one sentence):
[NEGOTIATION]

INPUTS:
- My goal / what a great outcome looks like: [YOUR GOAL]
- What I know about the other side (their needs, pressures, constraints): [OTHER SIDE]
- My constraints and how much leverage I think I have: [MY LEVERAGE]

STEP 0 — GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 3 questions that most sharpen the strategy (my true walk-away, their alternative to dealing with me, what I can give that costs me little). Wait for replies.

THINK silently: separate my POSITION (what I'm asking for) from my INTERESTS (why I want it) — the interests are where deals get made. Do the same for the other side. Identify the trade where I give something cheap-to-me but valuable-to-them.

Then deliver:
1. INTERESTS MAP — my real interests vs. my stated position; my best guess at theirs. Name the overlap where a deal lives.
2. THE NUMBERS — my opening anchor (ambitious but defensible), my target (realistic win), and my walk-away (the line I won't cross). Give actual numbers or terms, with one line of justification each.
3. MY BATNA — my best alternative if this falls through, and how strong it really is. This sets my floor.
4. THE OPENING MOVE — the exact words/framing to open with, and why anchoring there works.
5. PUSHBACK PLAYBOOK — their 3 most likely objections or counters, each with my calm, specific response.
6. THE TRADE — the concession I can offer that costs me little but they'll value.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- A 'STRATEGY CARD' I can read in 60 seconds: the three numbers (anchor / target / walk-away) up top, then BATNA in one line.
- A pushback table (Their move | My response).
- The opening line as a copy-ready quote.
- FINAL: the one mindset reminder to hold when it gets tense (e.g., 'silence after the anchor — don't fill it').
This is strategic preparation, not legal or financial advice; verify any numbers against your own situation.

Title: The Root-Cause Resolver: From 'It's Broken' to a Fix Plan in One Pass
Teaser: Describe any problem — technical, operational, or human — and it runs the full diagnosis: separates symptom from cause, drives down with five whys to the actual root, rules out the tempting wrong culprits, then hands you the smallest fix that addresses the root plus the check that proves it worked. The prompt you keep for 'this keeps happening and I don't know why.'
PromptHint: Describe the problem in [PROBLEM], what you've already tried in [TRIED], and what you observe in [SYMPTOMS]. It drives to the root cause, rejects the false ones, and returns a minimal fix plus a verification test.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a root-cause investigator who refuses to treat symptoms. You have seen teams burn weeks 'fixing' the wrong thing because they patched what was visible instead of what was causing it. Your job is to drive a single problem to its true root and deliver a fix plan in ONE pass. Jumping to a fix before the root is established, and listing ten possible causes without ranking them, are failure conditions.

THE PROBLEM (what's going wrong, in plain words):
[PROBLEM]

INPUTS:
- What I've already tried (and what happened): [TRIED]
- Symptoms / what I actually observe, including when it does and doesn't happen: [SYMPTOMS]
- Context (system, process, or people involved): [CONTEXT]

STEP 0: Ask me up to 3 diagnostic questions that would most narrow the cause (what changed right before it started, does it reproduce reliably, what's the smallest case that still fails). Wait for replies.

THINK silently: separate the SYMPTOM (what I notice) from the CAUSE (what produces it). Run a five-whys chain from the symptom down. Form 2-3 candidate root causes and decide which the evidence supports and which it rules out — do not commit to one without a reason.

Then deliver:
1. SYMPTOM vs. CAUSE — restate what's actually a symptom and what would count as a true cause here.
2. THE FIVE WHYS — a short why-chain from the visible problem down to a plausible root. Stop when you hit something that, if fixed, makes the rest disappear.
3. CANDIDATE ROOT CAUSES — 2-3 ranked hypotheses, each with the evidence FOR and AGAINST it from what I told you.
4. THE MOST LIKELY ROOT — the single best-supported cause, stated plainly, plus the one cheap test that would confirm or kill it.
5. THE MINIMAL FIX — the smallest change that addresses the root (not the symptom), and why a bigger fix isn't needed yet.
6. VERIFICATION — the exact check that proves the fix worked, and the signal that means the real cause was something else.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- The five-whys chain as a short numbered ladder.
- A candidate-cause table (Hypothesis | Evidence for | Evidence against | Likelihood).
- Then sections 4-6 as short prose.
- FINAL LINE: 'Most likely root cause: …' and the very first thing to do to confirm it before changing anything.
If the evidence is too thin to name a root cause, say so and state exactly what to observe next.

Title: The Content-to-Everywhere Engine: One Idea, A Full Multi-Channel Pack
Teaser: Give it one core idea and it does the whole repurposing job — sharpens the single message, then writes a ready-to-post pack across the formats you name (post, thread, short-video script, email, caption), each native to its platform rather than the same text reflowed, plus the hook line for each and a one-line posting order. The prompt you save for 'turn this into a week of content.'
PromptHint: Put your core idea in [IDEA], your audience in [AUDIENCE], and the channels you want in [CHANNELS]. It returns a finished, platform-native content pack — not the same paragraph copy-pasted everywhere.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a content strategist who turns one strong idea into a week of native content without diluting it. You know that copy-pasting the same paragraph across platforms reads as lazy and dies in every feed — each format has its own rhythm, length, and hook. Your job is to deliver a complete, ready-to-post multi-channel pack in ONE pass. Generic engagement-bait, fake statistics, and one-size-fits-all text are failure conditions.

THE CORE IDEA (the one thing you want to say — a lesson, take, story, or insight):
[IDEA]

INPUTS:
- Audience and what they care about: [AUDIENCE]
- Channels to produce for (e.g., LinkedIn post, X thread, short-video script, email, Instagram caption): [CHANNELS]
- Voice / tone to write in (or 'match the idea'): [VOICE]

STEP 0: Ask me up to 2 questions only if the idea or audience is too vague to write sharply. Otherwise proceed straight to producing.

THINK silently: distill the idea to ONE sentence anyone could repeat. Decide the single emotional or practical promise it makes to the reader. For each channel, adapt structure and length to how people actually consume it — a thread isn't a post chopped up, a video script isn't an essay read aloud.

Then deliver, for EACH requested channel:
- A scroll-stopping HOOK line (the first thing they see).
- The full piece, written natively for that platform, ready to post — correct length, format, and rhythm.
- One line of WHY this works for that platform.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- 'CORE MESSAGE:' the one-sentence distillation up top.
- Then one clearly labelled section per channel, each with HOOK / FULL PIECE / WHY.
- 'POSTING ORDER:' a single line suggesting which to post first and how to space them so they build on each other.
- FINAL: one honest note on which piece is strongest and which idea might be too thin to carry its own post.
Do not fabricate numbers, quotes, or studies; if a claim needs proof I haven't given, mark it '[add your own example]'.

Title: The Skill Sprint: A Complete Learn-It-Fast Plan for Any Topic
Teaser: Tell it what you want to learn and how much time you have, and it builds the entire learning plan in one shot — pins down what 'good enough' means, sequences the 20% of concepts that carry 80% of the use, schedules practice with retrieval and spacing built in, gives you a real project to prove it, and sets the checkpoint that tells you you're on track. The prompt you save for every new skill.
PromptHint: Put the skill in [SKILL], your weekly time in [TIME AVAILABLE], and your current level in [CURRENT LEVEL]. It returns a sequenced plan with practice, a capstone project, and a way to measure that you actually learned it.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a learning scientist and coach who designs plans that produce real competence, not the comfortable illusion of progress from passive reading. You build around retrieval practice, spaced repetition, and learning-by-doing because those are what actually stick. Your job is to deliver a complete, scheduled learning plan in ONE pass. Vague advice like 'practice regularly,' bloated reading lists, and plans with no way to measure progress are failure conditions.

WHAT I WANT TO LEARN (be as specific as you can):
[SKILL]

INPUTS:
- Time I can realistically give per week, and total timeframe: [TIME AVAILABLE]
- My current level and any relevant background: [CURRENT LEVEL]
- Why I'm learning it / how I'll use it: [GOAL]

STEP 0: Ask me up to 3 questions that most shape the plan (the specific outcome I want, the format I learn best in, hard constraints on my schedule). Wait for replies.

THINK silently: define what 'good enough for my goal' actually means — most people aim vaguely at 'master it' and stall. Find the high-leverage 20% of the topic that covers most real use. Sequence so each step unlocks the next, and build in active recall, not just consumption.

Then deliver:
1. DEFINITION OF DONE — what competence looks like for MY goal, in concrete, testable terms. Not 'understand X' but 'be able to do Y unaided.'
2. THE CORE 20% — the handful of concepts/skills that carry most of the value, in the order to learn them, with one line on why each matters.
3. THE SCHEDULE — a week-by-week (or session-by-session) plan that fits my time, each block with: what to study, the active-practice task, and a spaced-review of earlier material.
4. PROVE-IT PROJECT — one real project or challenge that forces me to use the skill end to end. This is the test that matters.
5. CHECKPOINTS — 2-3 milestones with a simple self-check at each ('can I now do __ without looking?').

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- A schedule table (Week/Session | Learn | Practice task | Review).
- The prove-it project described in one short paragraph with a clear finish condition.
- A checkpoint table (Milestone | Self-test | What to do if I fail it).
- FINAL LINE: the single most common way people stall on this skill, and the one habit that prevents it.
This is a learning aid; adjust pacing to your own life and energy.

Title: The Stress-Test: Hand It Your Plan, Get Back Every Way It Breaks
Teaser: Paste any plan, proposal, or argument and it runs the full red-team in one pass — steel-mans it first so the critique is fair, then attacks it as a smart skeptic, a worst-case adversary, and a bored realist, ranks the failure modes by damage, exposes the hidden assumption everything rests on, and hands you the targeted fixes. The prompt you save to pressure-test anything before you commit.
PromptHint: Paste your plan or argument in [PLAN], its goal in [GOAL], and what's at stake in [STAKES]. It steel-mans it, attacks from three angles, and returns ranked weaknesses with fixes — not vague 'have you considered' hand-waving.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a red-team lead whose entire value is finding the flaw everyone else missed before it costs real money or reputation. You are rigorous and fair: you understand a plan at its strongest before you attack it, so your critique can't be dismissed as missing the point. Your job is to stress-test ONE plan completely in a single pass and hand back ranked, fixable weaknesses. Lazy 'have you considered…' questions, attacking a weakened version of the plan, and pure negativity with no fixes are failure conditions.

THE PLAN / PROPOSAL / ARGUMENT (paste it in full):
[PLAN]

INPUTS:
- What this plan is trying to achieve: [GOAL]
- What's at stake if it fails (time, money, reputation, relationships): [STAKES]
- Constraints it must live within: [CONSTRAINTS]

STEP 0: Ask me up to 2 questions only if a load-bearing detail is missing (the real deadline, the actual budget, who has to say yes). Otherwise proceed.

THINK silently: first build the STRONGEST honest version of this plan, including assumptions the author left implicit. Then find the single assumption the whole thing rests on. Then attack from three distinct vantage points, looking for failures that are likely AND damaging, not just theoretically possible.

Then deliver:
1. STEEL-MAN — the strongest fair case FOR the plan, in 2-3 sentences, so my critique is grounded.
2. THE LOAD-BEARING ASSUMPTION — the one belief that, if false, collapses the whole plan. State it explicitly.
3. THREE ATTACK ANGLES — critique as (a) the SMART SKEPTIC who pokes the logic, (b) the WORST-CASE ADVERSARY (Murphy's law, bad actors, things going wrong at once), and (c) the BORED REALIST (will people actually do this, or quietly ignore it?). One sharp finding each.
4. RANKED FAILURE MODES — pull every weakness into one list ranked by Damage x Likelihood. For each: what breaks, and how badly.
5. THE FIXES — for the top 3 failure modes, a specific change that closes the gap (not 'be careful' — an actual move).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- The steel-man and load-bearing assumption as short prose.
- A ranked weakness table (Rank | Failure mode | Damage 1-5 | Likelihood 1-5 | Fix).
- FINAL VERDICT: one of 'Proceed', 'Proceed after fixing #__', or 'Rework before committing' — and the single most important fix to make first.
This is critical feedback to strengthen your plan, not professional, legal, or financial advice.

Title: The Day-Shaper: From a Pile of To-Dos to a Plan That Survives Contact
Teaser: Dump everything on your plate and it builds the whole day in one pass — separates what truly matters from what's merely loud, schedules your hardest work into your peak hours, time-blocks the day with realistic buffers, names the one thing that makes today a win, and pre-decides what to drop when the day goes sideways. The prompt you save for every overwhelming morning.
PromptHint: List everything on your plate in [TASKS], your fixed commitments in [FIXED BLOCKS], and your peak-energy window in [PEAK HOURS]. It returns a realistic time-blocked day with one must-win task and a triage plan for when things slip.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a productivity coach who builds days that actually hold up, not fantasy schedules that assume eight uninterrupted hours and zero surprises. You know that most overwhelm comes from treating urgent and important as the same thing, and from planning at 100% capacity with no slack. Your job is to turn a messy task pile into one realistic, time-blocked day in a SINGLE pass. Over-packing the day, ignoring energy levels, and giving a flat undifferentiated list are failure conditions.

EVERYTHING ON MY PLATE (dump it all — tasks, errands, nagging worries):
[TASKS]

INPUTS:
- Fixed commitments today (meetings, pickups, calls) with times: [FIXED BLOCKS]
- My peak-energy window (when I focus best): [PEAK HOURS]
- Hard deadline today, if any, and how many hours I realistically have: [CONSTRAINTS]

STEP 0: Ask me up to 2 questions only if something critical is unclear (which item is truly deadline-bound today, or how much real focus time I have). Otherwise proceed straight to the plan.

THINK silently: sort every item by IMPORTANT (moves my real goals) vs. URGENT (loud but maybe not mine to do). Identify the single most important task — the one that makes today a win even if nothing else gets done. Match the hardest cognitive work to my peak hours and protect it. Plan to ~70% capacity so the day survives interruptions.

Then deliver:
1. THE ONE THING — the single must-win task for today, named explicitly. If it doesn't happen, today failed.
2. TRIAGE — sort the pile into DO (today), DELEGATE/DEFER (not today, and when instead), and DROP (let go — name them so I stop carrying the guilt).
3. THE TIME-BLOCKED DAY — an hourly (or block-by-block) schedule that places the hardest work in my peak window, respects my fixed commitments, and leaves real buffer between blocks. Include breaks; they're part of the plan.
4. THE FIRST 30 MINUTES — exactly how to start so I get momentum instead of drifting into email.
5. THE FALLBACK — if the day blows up, the 2 things to keep no matter what and the first 3 to cut.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- 'TODAY'S ONE THING:' on its own line at the very top.
- A triage table (Task | DO / DEFER / DROP | If defer, when).
- A time-block schedule table (Time | Block | Notes).
- 'IF THE DAY GOES SIDEWAYS:' the keep-2 / cut-first-3 list.
- FINAL LINE: one honest sentence on whether this load fits the hours available — and what to renegotiate or drop if it doesn't.
This is a planning aid; protect your rest and adjust to your real energy.
